title: Isaiah 11
subtitle: The Shoot from the Stump of Jesse

sections.header: Verses 1–9 — “The Wolf Will Live with the Lamb”

heb.paragraph: "A shoot (ḥōṭer) will come up from the stump of Jesse; from his roots a Branch will bear fruit" (v.1). The Spirit of the LORD rests on him: wisdom, understanding, counsel, might, knowledge, fear of the LORD — six attributes, or seven if the fear of the LORD is the crown (v.2). He judges with righteousness; with the breath of his lips he slays the wicked (v.4). The wolf lives with the lamb, the leopard lies down with the goat, a little child leads them (v.6). Nothing harms or destroys on all God’s holy mountain; the earth is full of the knowledge of the LORD as the waters cover the sea (v.9).

panels.ctx: The stump of Jesse (not David) is significant: the dynasty is cut back to its pre-royal root. The Messiah emerges not from Davidic glory but from Davidic ruin. The seven-fold Spirit (v.2) is traditionally counted as: wisdom, understanding, counsel, might, knowledge, fear of the LORD (with fear appearing twice in v.3, creating seven). Revelation 5:6 describes the Lamb with "seven spirits" — possibly drawing on this Isaiah text. The peace-kingdom vision (vv.6–9) is the most beloved utopian text in the OT: predator and prey reconciled, creation at peace, a child safe among lions.

cross.note: The Spirit descending on Jesus at baptism — the fulfilment of the Spirit resting on the shoot (11:2).
The Root of Jesse will spring up; in him the Gentiles will hope — Paul quotes Isa 11:10.
The Lion of the tribe of Judah, the Root of David — Revelation’s titles draw from Isa 11:1, 10.
The river of life; no more curse — the new creation that fulfils Isa 11:6–9’s peace kingdom.
Creation itself will be liberated from its bondage to decay — the cosmic restoration Isa 11 envisions.

notes.note: A shoot from the stump: not from David’s throne but from Jesse’s root. The dynasty cut back to its origin. From the ruin, the Messiah.
The Spirit of the LORD rests on him: wisdom, understanding, counsel, might, knowledge, fear of the LORD. The seven-fold anointing. Matt 3:16–17 fulfils.
He judges with righteousness, not by what he sees or hears. He slays the wicked with the breath of his lips. The word IS the weapon.
Wolf and lamb. Leopard and goat. Calf and lion. A little child leads. "The earth will be full of the knowledge of the LORD as the waters cover the sea." The most complete peace-vision in the Bible.

Calvin reads the stump as essential: the Messiah comes not from Davidic glory but from Davidic humiliation. The dynasty must be reduced to nothing before the shoot appears. This is the cross-principle: exaltation through humiliation.

Calvin interprets the animal-peace as both literal (the creation will be restored) and figurative (violent humans will be tamed by the gospel). Both readings coexist: the gospel transforms hearts now; the new creation transforms nature later.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes "stump" (gezaʿ) implies the tree has been CUT DOWN. The Davidic dynasty is not just weakened but felled. The shoot’s emergence from a stump is resurrection in agricultural metaphor.
The NET observes the four predator-prey pairs (wolf/lamb, leopard/goat, calf/lion, cow/bear) systematically dismantle the natural order of violence. The peace is comprehensive, not selective.

Oswalt reads the passage as the climax of the Immanuel section (chs.7–12): the child of 7:14 and 9:6 now receives the Spirit, governs with justice, and establishes cosmic peace. The messianic portrait is complete.

Oswalt: the peace-kingdom reverses Eden’s curse (Gen 3:14–19). The enmity between serpent and woman’s seed is resolved: the infant plays safely by the cobra’s den. The Messiah undoes what Adam’s sin caused.

Childs reads the passage as the canonical answer to the question raised by 6:13 ("the holy seed is the stump"): what grows from the stump? The shoot of Jesse. The stump-theology finds its resolution here.
Childs notes "the earth full of the knowledge of the LORD as waters cover the sea" is the eschatological vision toward which the entire book moves. Hab 2:14 quotes it; the NT inherits it.

heb.paragraph: "In that day the Root of Jesse will stand as a banner (nēs) for the peoples; the nations will rally to him" (v.10). The Lord will reach out his hand a SECOND time to reclaim the remnant — from Assyria, Egypt, Cush, Elam, Babylonia, Hamath, and the islands of the Mediterranean (v.11). He will gather the exiles of Israel and the scattered people of Judah from the four quarters of the earth (v.12). Ephraim and Judah reconciled (v.13). The sea dried up as at the exodus (v.15). A highway from Assyria, as there was for Israel when they came up from Egypt (v.16).

panels.ctx: The "second time" (v.11) explicitly invokes the exodus as the pattern: what God did once (delivering from Egypt), he will do again (delivering from exile). The geographical scope (Assyria, Egypt, Cush, Elam, Babylonia, Hamath, islands) covers the entire known world — the regathering is universal. The reunion of Ephraim (northern) and Judah (southern) reverses the division of 1 Kings 12. The highway motif (v.16) becomes central in Isaiah (35:8; 40:3; 62:10). Rom 15:12 quotes v.10 for the inclusion of the Gentiles.

cross.note: The Root of Jesse will spring up; in him the Gentiles will hope — Paul quotes v.10 as the scriptural basis for Gentile inclusion.
God divided the sea — the first exodus that vv.15–16 explicitly parallel.
A highway will be there, the Way of Holiness — the highway theme expanded.
Prepare the way for the LORD — the highway of the second exodus.
A great multitude from every nation — the fulfilment of the universal gathering of 11:10–12.

notes.note: The Root of Jesse as a banner for the nations. Not just Israel’s king but the world’s rallying point. Rom 15:12 quotes this.
The SECOND exodus: God’s hand reaches out again. From Assyria, Egypt, Cush, Elam, Babylonia, islands. From the four corners.
Ephraim and Judah reconciled. The 200-year division healed. North and south become one people again.
The sea dried up; a highway from Assyria. The exodus pattern repeated. What God did once, he does again.
Calvin reads the "banner for the peoples" as the gospel proclaimed to all nations: Christ is the rallying point not just for Israel but for every people. The scope is universal from the beginning.
Calvin: the second exodus is the pattern for all subsequent deliverances. Every time God rescues his people, he reenacts the exodus. The pattern is permanent.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes "Root of Jesse" shifts from "shoot FROM the stump" (v.1) to "Root OF Jesse" — the Messiah is both Jesse’s descendant and Jesse’s origin. Rev 22:16: "I am the Root and Offspring of David."
The NET lists the geographic spread: east (Elam, Babylonia), south (Egypt, Cush), north (Assyria, Hamath), west (islands). The compass is complete.

Oswalt reads the second-exodus theme as the bridge between First and Second Isaiah: ch.11 announces it; chs.40–55 develop it in detail. The concept of a new exodus is arguably Isaiah’s most influential contribution to NT theology (Mark 1:2–3; Luke 9:31).
Oswalt: the highway motif transforms geography into theology. A physical road becomes a metaphor for God’s provision of a way home. The highway IS the grace.

Childs reads the passage as the conclusion of the Immanuel section: the messianic king (11:1–9) draws the nations (v.10) and regathers Israel (vv.11–16). The section that began with Ahaz’s fear (ch.7) ends with universal hope (ch.11).
Childs notes the "highway from Assyria" anticipates 40:3 ("prepare the way of the LORD"). The highway-theology is Isaiah’s literary and theological spine.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVThe wolf will live with the lamb, the leopard will lie down with the goat, the calf and the lion and the yearling together; and a little child will lead them.ESVThe wolf shall dwell with the lamb, and the leopard shall lie down with the young goat, and the calf and the lion and the fattened calf together; and a little child shall lead them.NIVA shoot will come up from the stump of Jesse; from his roots a Branch will bear fruit.ESVThere shall come forth a shoot from the stump of Jesse, and a branch from his roots shall bear fruit.

src.title: The Cyrus Cylinder (539 BC)
src.quote: A clay cylinder in which Cyrus of Persia describes his conquest of Babylon and his policy of returning captive peoples to their homelands — the Persian policy that fulfilled Isaiah 44:28–45:1.
src.note: The cylinder confirms Isaiah’s prophecy of a Persian king who would liberate captives and authorize temple rebuilding, whether the prophecy is dated early (8th c.) or late (6th c.).

src.title: The Sennacherib Prism (c.700 BC)
src.quote: Sennacherib’s own account of his 701 BC campaign states he shut Hezekiah up “like a bird in a cage” in Jerusalem — but conspicuously does NOT claim to have captured the city.
src.note: The silence confirms Isaiah 36–37: Sennacherib besieged but never conquered Jerusalem. His own records inadvertently verify Isaiah’s narrative of divine deliverance.

src.title: The Great Isaiah Scroll (1QIsaᵃ, Dead Sea Scrolls)
src.quote: A complete copy of Isaiah found at Qumran, dated c.125 BC. The oldest complete biblical manuscript, it is virtually identical to the Masoretic text 1,000 years later.
src.note: The scroll demonstrates the remarkable textual stability of Isaiah across a millennium. The text the church reads today is essentially the text the Qumran community read.

rec.text: "A shoot from the stump: not from David’s throne but from Jesse’s root. The dynasty cut back to its origin. From the ruin, the Messiah."

rows.label: The Second Exodus — 11:10–16
rows.text: Root of Jesse as BANNER for nations (Rom 15:12). Second exodus. Ephraim and Judah RECONCILED. A HIGHWAY.

hebtext.note: "A shoot (ḥōṭer) will come up from the stump of Jesse; from his roots a Branch will bear fruit" (v.1). The Spirit of the LORD rests on him: wisdom, understanding, counsel, might, knowledge, fear of the LORD — six attributes, or seven if the fear of the LORD is the crown (v.2). He judges with righteousness; with the breath of his lips he slays the wicked (v.4). The wolf lives with the lamb, the leopard lies down with the goat, a little child leads them (v.6). Nothing harms or destroys on all God’s holy mountain; the earth is full of the knowledge of the LORD as the waters cover the sea (v.9).

"In that day the Root of Jesse will stand as a banner (nēs) for the peoples; the nations will rally to him" (v.10). The Lord will reach out his hand a SECOND time to reclaim the remnant — from Assyria, Egypt, Cush, Elam, Babylonia, Hamath, and the islands of the Mediterranean (v.11). He will gather the exiles of Israel and the scattered people of Judah from the four quarters of the earth (v.12). Ephraim and Judah reconciled (v.13). The sea dried up as at the exodus (v.15). A highway from Assyria, as there was for Israel when they came up from Egypt (v.16).

themes.note: Prophecy peaks at 10: the shoot from the stump, the seven-fold Spirit, the peace kingdom. Mission at 9: Root of Jesse as banner for all nations (Rom 15:12). The most complete messianic portrait in the OT.
